schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch date the song to David's later years and lay out its two-fold structure with introductory and closing strophes.
Matthew Henry draws four lessons: God's people have many foes, God is a present help, mercies deserve glory, and thanks should be speedy.
Gill refers the exposition to Psalm 18, the song's near-identical twin.
Clarke heads the whole as thanksgiving carrying prophetic declarations about the Messiah's humiliation and exaltation.
The heaped titles are declarations, not vocatives; Ellicott notes the recension lacks the Psalm's opening line 'I will love thee, O LORD.'
Henry turns the predicates into worship: David adores God's perfections and triumphs in his covenant interest, 'if my God, then my rock.'
Gill refers the string of titles to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
Ellicott notes the recension fuses 'my God, my rock' into 'the God of my rock' and adds the clause 'my refuge, my saviour' absent from the Psalm.
Gill makes 'in him will I trust' the hinge of the confession, referring it to the parallel exposition.
Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as settled confidence: calling on the praised God issues of course in deliverance from enemies.
Henry makes it the improvement of interest in God: what deserves praise draws us to prayer and glory.
Gill lets the verse stand as the pledge answering the opening titles.

themes: the God worthy to be praised

schools.summary: K&D prefer 'breakers of death' for the parallelism; Ellicott notes the deliberate variation from the Psalm's 'bands of death.'
Clarke: in a primary sense the waves of death belong to David, but 'generally and fully' to the Messiah alone.
Gill takes the waves and floods as the overwhelming assaults of ungodly men, referring to the parallel.

debates.question: Do the 'waves of death' describe David's peril, or the Messiah's?

sides.position: primarily David's dangers, chiefly under Saul and Absalom
primarily David, yet fully and properly the Messiah alone

schools.summary: K&D read the cords of hell and snares of death as the whole abyss opening under a man engulfed, out of which only God can reach.
Henry presses the helplessness: David could no more free himself than a man in the grave, so he turns to prayer.
Gill reads the imagery of the encompassing dangers and terrors that beset the persecuted saint.
Ellicott notes the two verbs 'called' and 'cried' are here identical, and the elliptical 'my cry in his ears' is filled out in the Psalm.
Henry makes it the model of importunate prayer, God heard 'out of his temple in heaven' where he is continually adored.
Gill rests on the plain sense, the cry of the afflicted heard in heaven.
The descent is modelled on Sinai and Deborah's song; K&D and Ellicott weigh 'foundations of heaven' against the Psalm's 'of the hills.'
Henry reads the Sinai-metaphors as figures of God's glory, wrath, confusion of the foe, and readiness, not literal thunder in David's battles.
Gill refers the convulsion to the parallel exposition, the shaking a token of God's anger.
K&D keep vv.9-11 as the expansion of the descent, the smoke, fire, and coals picturing the Sinai-like anger of the descending God.
Henry names the verse the chief figure of God's displeasure against David's enemies: 'Who knows the power and terror of his wrath?'
Gill refers the burning imagery to the parallel, reading it of God's anger against the foe.
Gill refers the bowing of the heavens to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
Henry reads the descent as God's readiness to help, hastening to David's succour though he had seemed far off, yet coming as a God that hides himself.
Clarke, following Kennicott, argues the whole ode fits the Messiah alone, not David, since Paul quotes it of the Gentiles' conversion.
K&D explain 'riding on the cherub' from the enthronement above the ark; Ellicott notes 'he was seen' where the Psalm reads 'he did fly.'
Gill refers the cherub-flight to his exposition of the parallel.

debates.question: Whose song is this — David's, the Messiah's, or David's as a type of Christ?

sides.position: the Messiah alone; David is excluded even from a double sense
David the singer, describing his own deliverance in borrowed Sinai splendour
David's real history, foreshadowing Christ as its type

outliers.note: With Kennicott, insists the ode 'relates only to the Messiah' and that David is excluded from even the double sense.

schools.summary: K&D read the rare word as a 'gathering together' of water, God the cloud-gatherer; Ellicott notes a word dropped out that the Psalm preserves.
Henry: God makes darkness his pavilion, for the amazement of enemies and the protection of his people.
Gill refers the pavilion of darkness to the parallel exposition.
K&D take the coals as lightning breaking from the clouds and note the recension is 'terribly mutilated'; Ellicott reads 'out of the brightness' and prefers the Psalm's fuller form.
Gill refers the fiery imagery to the parallel, the coals of God's wrath against his enemies.
K&D read the thunder as God's weapon, the verb 'discomfited' being the standing word for foes routed by divine interposition; Ellicott weighs 'from heaven' against the Psalm.
Gill refers the thunder to his exposition of the parallel psalm.

themes: the voice of God as thunder

schools.summary: K&D read the arrows as lightning loosed on the enemy amid thunder, the verb 'discomfited' again marking a divinely-wrought rout.
Henry names the arrows among the figures of God's displeasure: who can stand before God when he is angry?
Gill refers the arrows and lightning to the parallel, the terror of God on his servant's foes.
K&D read the bared deep as uncovering the abyss into which the rescued man had fallen; Ellicott weighs 'channels of the sea' and the shift from 'his' to 'thy' in the Psalm.
Henry gathers the verse with v.8 as the figure of the confusion into which David's enemies were thrown.
Gill refers the uncovering of the deep to the parallel exposition.

themes: the foundations of the world laid bare

schools.summary: K&D: God stretched his hand from the height into the abyss and drew out the sinking man; 'many waters' is the peril, not the enemy.
Gill refers the drawing-out to the parallel, the many waters being the manifold troubles from which God delivered David.

themes: rescue as the purpose of the storm

schools.summary: K&D read the verse as David's frank confession that the foe was mightier than he, so the deliverance is wholly God's.
Henry names the strong enemy as Saul, who but for God would have been too hard for David.
Gill refers the deliverance to the parallel, the strong enemy being Saul and every mighty adversary.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel, the enemies falling on David in his day of weakness.
Henry: 'ready to sink, the Lord is my stay, by whom I am supported' — the God confessed in calm proved in the storm.
Gill refers the enlargement and deliverance to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
Henry: deliverance from covenant-love, not common providence; 'he delighted in me' makes David a type of Christ whom God upheld (Isaiah 42:1).
K&D: David's 'righteousness' is not perfect holiness but the rightness of his aims and deeds against his persecutors' wickedness.
Henry: recompensed not 'for' but 'according to' his righteousness — of grace, not debt; his integrity chiefly toward Saul and the rebels.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D place the verse in the self-vindication strophe: settled fidelity, not sinless perfection, the man not turning from God in high-handed apostasy.
Henry: David took false steps but never deserted God; grace kept him from presumptuous, though not from infirmity's, sins.
Gill refers the profession of fidelity to the parallel exposition.

themes: keeping the ways of the LORD

schools.summary: Ellicott: this text reads 'I did not depart from his statutes' where the Psalm has 'I did not put away his statutes,' a slight deliberate variation.
Henry: David made God's word his rule, keeping his judgments before him and his statutes even in exile, carrying his religion with him.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
Clarke and Kennicott: 'kept myself from mine iniquity' suits a sinless Messiah once read as 'iniquities' (plural) per the Syriac, Arabic, and Chaldee.
Henry: David kept watch over his own besetting sin, notably in sparing Saul, the matter of Uriah standing as the confessed exception.
Gill refers the profession of uprightness to his exposition of the parallel.

debates.question: Can 'I have kept myself from mine iniquity' be spoken by the Messiah?

sides.position: yes — read 'iniquities' with the versions; the context shows a sinless speaker
it is David's watchfulness over his own sin, with Uriah the confessed exception

schools.summary: K&D note the recension's unexampled word for 'cleanness'; Ellicott sees 'to my cleanness' simplifying the Psalm's 'cleanness of my hands.'
Gill refers the closing note of self-vindication to his exposition of the parallel.
K&D open the strophe on God's reflexive 'showing himself' merciful, upright, pure — answering each man according to his character.
Henry: 'as we are found towards God, he will be found towards us'; his mercy the joy of the merciful, his uprightness of the upright.
Gill refers the rule of correspondence to his exposition of the parallel.
Ellicott corrects 'unsavoury' to 'froward'; K&D read the bold form as reflexive — God baffling the crooked rather than himself being perverse.
Henry: God wrestles with the froward and they are foiled — 'Woe unto him that strives with his Maker!'
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D mark the verse as the hinge into the psalm's second half — David one of the 'afflicted people'; Ellicott weighs the wording against the Psalm.
Henry: God saves the afflicted out of or through their afflictions, but his eye marks the haughty to be brought down, for he resists the proud.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D open the psalm's second half here, God himself the lamp; Ellicott gathers the 'lamp' passages that betoken an enduring house and kingdom.
Henry: to the benighted, at-a-loss believer, God is himself the lamp to show the way and dispel the darkness.
Gill refers the lamp to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D: in God's strength David runs on troops and leaps walls, overcoming every hostile power; they defend 'run' against 'smash.'
Henry: God, calling David to fight his battles, qualified him for them, so the running and leaping are the fruit of divine equipping.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D: David grounds confidence on God's acts and word — his way perfect, his word refined silver, a shield to all who trust (cf. Proverbs 30:5).
Henry contrasts God's tried word with man's broken promises: God will finish his work where men leave off half-done.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.

themes: a buckler to all who trust

schools.summary: K&D: none is God or Rock but Jehovah, echoing the Song of Moses and Hannah; 'rock' recalls the psalm's opening title.
Henry: all other deities are counterfeits and dead, but the Lord liveth; none is to be relied on but he.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.

themes: who is a rock save our God

schools.summary: Ellicott prefers 'my strong fortress' and notes the Psalm's different 'girdeth me with strength'; K&D read God as the guide who leads the upright.
Henry: God made David's way perfect, giving success in all his undertakings.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
Henry: God made David swift like a hind, of use in charging and retreating, and set him on high places for safety and dignity.
K&D keep the verse with vv.33-35 as part of the equipment for victory God bestows.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
Henry: God taught David's hands to war, his dexterity as good as strength, so the bow of steel is bent by skill more than force.
K&D render 'my arms span brazen bows,' reading it of the martial competence God imparts.
K&D and Ellicott read the word as God's 'hearkening' or 'answering' to prayer; the Psalm adds 'thy right hand hath holden me up.'
Henry keeps 'gentleness': God's grace and tender mercy made David great, gave him wealth, authority, and a great name.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D: God gives the walker a broad space, removing obstructions, so the feet do not slip — the continuation of divine hearkening.
Henry gathers it with v.20 as the liberty God gave: David's steps enlarged, no longer straitened and confined.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D read the verbs as reaching into the future — record and prophecy of expanding dominion; the recension loses an Exodus 15 echo the Psalm keeps.
Henry: David's were speedy and complete victories — 'I turned not again till I had consumed them.'
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D keep the future edge — foes so broken they cannot rise; Ellicott notes the recension's heightening and the stronger 'crushed them.'
Henry heaps the language of finality: the foes wounded, consumed, trampled, disabled to rise, their necks at David's mercy.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D keep the verse in the victory-strophe with a forward reach; the recension places 'God girdeth me with strength' here rather than at v.33.
Henry: David gives God the glory of all his courage and ability — the girding and the subduing alike are God's act.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D read 'thou makest mine enemies turn the back' — the routed foe handed over, the victory thorough and God-given.
Henry: the enemies disabled to rise, their necks at David's mercy — the token of complete conquest.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D: the foe looks out even to their gods and finds none; Ellicott notes 'they looked' becomes 'they cried' in the Psalm by a single letter.
Henry: the enemies cried to earth and heaven in vain, unanswered because they sought God only at the last extremity and as enemies still.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D and Ellicott compare the recension's 'dust of the earth' with the Psalm's more vivid 'dust before the wind,' a designed variation.
Henry: David beat them small as dust scattered by the wind and trodden by every foot — reduced to the lightest and lowest.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D read 'strivings of my people' of David's domestic foes — Ishbosheth, Absalom, Sheba — his rescue at home the road to rule over the nations.
Henry: David reserved to rule Israel and be head of the heathen, foreshadowing the Son of David who goes on conquering (Revelation 6:2).
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D note the reflexive 'made themselves flatterers' and 'upon hearsay they obey'; Ellicott notes the clauses are transposed in the Psalm.
Henry: strangers forced to submit, though feignedly, out of fear at the report of David's victories.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
Ellicott weighs 'came trembling' against 'came limping' from their fastnesses; K&D render 'despair, and tremble out of their castles.'
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D: 'the LORD liveth' is a declaration, not a wish; to bless God is to ascribe him his own perfections — praise adds nothing to him.
Henry: against the dead, disappointing idols, David exults in the living God, the Rock of his salvation.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D render 'the God who giveth me vengeance and bringeth nations under me'; the vengeance is God's righteous act through David, not private revenge.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D render 'who leadeth me out, exalteth me, delivereth me from the man of violence,' noting the recension's singular 'violent man.'
Henry: David lifted as high as the throne above his foes; the violent man is Saul and every like adversary.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
K&D: the grace to David overflows Israel's bounds; Paul quotes the verse (Romans 15:9) with Deut 32:43 and Psalm 117 for the Gentiles' praise.
Henry: David resolves to give thanks publicly, unashamed to own the God of Israel even among the heathen.
Gill refers the verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.
Ellicott weighs 'tower of salvation' against 'great deliverance giveth he' and reads the psalm's hope of the perpetual kingdom; K&D deny the recension is a superior Davidic revision.
Henry: mercy to David's anointed reaches to his seed for ever, resting on 2 Samuel 7:15 and terminating in Christ, David's seed for evermore.
Gill refers the closing verse to his exposition of the parallel psalm.

debates.question: Does the song's close speak of David, the Messiah, or both in the covenant seed?

sides.position: of David and his line, historically, in the perpetual-kingdom promise
of David as type, terminating in Christ his seed for evermore
